raw_response_text:
<features>
- fur color: black, brown, gray
- fur length: long
- ear shape: erect
- eye color: not visible
</features>
<kb>
- IF [fur color = black AND fur color = brown AND fur length = long AND ear shape = erect] THEN [36]
- IF [fur color = white AND fur color = brown AND fur color = black AND eye color = blue] THEN [6]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- The rule for class 36 fires for the Query Image because it has black, brown, and long fur, and erect ears.
</rule_check>

<response>36</response>

raw_response_text:
<features>
- long fur: present
- blue eyes: absent
- black nose: present
- black ears: present
- white and brown fur: present
</features>
<kb>
- IF [long fur = present AND blue eyes = absent AND black nose = present AND black ears = present AND white and brown fur = present] THEN [6]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- The rule for class 6 fires for the Query Image because it has long fur, a black nose, black ears, and white and brown fur, and does not have blue eyes.
</rule_check>

<response>6</response>

Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>

- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>

referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>
